title: React JSX – JavaScript XML Deep Dive

meta.title: React JSX – Complete Guide to JavaScript XML (2026)
meta.description: Master JSX in React - syntax, expressions, conditional rendering, lists, fragments, and best practices. Learn how JSX transforms to React elements.

content.value: JSX (JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for JavaScript that allows you to write HTML-like code directly in JavaScript. It makes React code more readable and expressive. JSX gets compiled to regular JavaScript function calls using React.createElement.

value: **Use parentheses for multi-line JSX** – Improves readability
**Keep expressions simple** – Extract complex logic outside JSX
**Use fragments to avoid extra DOM nodes** – Reduces nesting
**Always add keys when mapping** – Helps React identify items
**Use camelCase for all HTML attributes** – Consistent with React
**Self-close tags with no children** – Cleaner syntax
**Use template literals for dynamic strings** – Instead of concatenation
**Extract repeated JSX into components** – DRY principle
**Use boolean attributes without values** – Cleaner when true
**Avoid inline functions in render** – Extract to handlers

content.value: JSX is a powerful syntax extension that makes React code more readable and maintainable. It combines the power of JavaScript with the familiarity of HTML. Understanding JSX fundamentals, expressions, conditional rendering, and common patterns is essential for effective React development.

faq.question: What does JSX stand for?
faq.answer: JavaScript XML - a syntax extension for JavaScript.

faq.question: Is JSX required for React?
faq.answer: No, you can use React.createElement directly, but JSX is recommended.

faq.question: Why className instead of class?
faq.answer: class is a reserved word in JavaScript, so React uses className.

faq.question: Can browsers understand JSX?
faq.answer: No, JSX must be compiled to JavaScript using Babel or TypeScript.
